blurb: Paxos, Raft, leader election, fencing, split-brain.

questions.q: What's the minimum number of nodes in a Raft cluster to tolerate 1 failure?
questions.explanation: Quorum = ⌊N/2⌋ + 1. With N=3, quorum=2, tolerates 1 failure. With N=2, losing 1 leaves only 1 — no quorum. Raft clusters are almost always odd-numbered.

questions.q: Random election timeouts in Raft exist to prevent what?
questions.explanation: If all followers timeout simultaneously, they all become candidates, each votes for itself, no one wins → new election. Random 150–300ms timeouts stagger the transitions.

questions.q: A client acquires a distributed lock with 30-second TTL. The client process pauses for 45 seconds (GC). A second client acquires the lock. What prevents both from writing?
choices: Fencing tokens (monotonic ID per lock acquisition)
questions.explanation: Fencing: each lock acquisition returns a monotonic token. Target storage rejects writes with a lower token than last seen. Even if two clients believe they hold the lock, only the latest's writes commit.

questions.q: What's 'split brain' in a replicated system?
choices: Dual leaders due to network partition, each accepting writes
questions.explanation: Split brain: two nodes each believe they're the leader (usually across a partition), both accept conflicting writes. The GitHub 2018 outage is a canonical example.

questions.q: CAP theorem: during a partition, a CP system chooses to sacrifice what?
questions.explanation: CP: during partition, one side stops serving writes (or reads) to preserve consistency. Spanner is CP. Dynamo/Cassandra are AP — serve stale rather than fail.

questions.q: Leader leases (time-bounded leadership certificates) are used to avoid what problem?
choices: Stale reads from a former leader that hasn't noticed it's been deposed
questions.explanation: A deposed leader with stale state might still serve reads. Leases bound its authority: after N seconds, it cannot serve authoritatively without re-confirming from a quorum.

questions.q: Byzantine fault tolerance (BFT) vs crash fault tolerance (CFT). What makes BFT harder?
choices: Nodes may actively lie / return malicious responses — not just crash
questions.explanation: CFT (Raft, Paxos) assumes crashed nodes stop responding. BFT assumes nodes may send corrupt/malicious messages. BFT needs 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f byzantine failures vs 2f+1 for crash faults.

questions.q: The Two Generals problem proves what about distributed systems?
choices: No finite protocol over lossy channels guarantees common knowledge
questions.explanation: No protocol over an unreliable channel can make both generals certain the other has received the plan. Extends to: no distributed commit protocol is exactly atomic in the face of message loss — 2PC blocks, 3PC timeouts, etc.

questions.q: Your 3-node etcd cluster loses two nodes simultaneously during a data center power event. The surviving node has all the data. Can it serve reads and writes?
choices: Yes — it has all the data
Reads only in stale mode; no writes — quorum (2 of 3) is lost
Full read/write — single node is sufficient
questions.explanation: etcd (Raft) requires a quorum of N/2+1 nodes. With 1 of 3, there's no quorum. The surviving node cannot elect itself leader or commit new writes. It can serve linearizable reads only if it was already leader with a valid lease, otherwise only serializable (potentially stale) reads.

questions.q: A ZooKeeper ensemble has 5 nodes across two data centers: 3 in DC-A, 2 in DC-B. A network partition isolates the DCs. Which side can still serve writes?
choices: Neither — ZooKeeper shuts down on partition
questions.explanation: Quorum for 5 nodes is 3. DC-A has 3 nodes and can form a quorum, so it elects a leader and continues serving reads and writes. DC-B with only 2 nodes cannot form a quorum and stops accepting writes. This is why odd-numbered, partition-aware placement matters.

questions.q: Your Raft leader is processing a client write. It appends to its own log and sends AppendEntries to followers. Two of four followers ACK, but then the leader crashes before responding to the client. What happens to that write?
choices: Write is committed — a majority (leader + 2 followers = 3 of 5) had it; the new leader will include it
questions.explanation: In Raft, an entry is committed once a majority has it in their logs. The leader plus 2 followers = 3 of 5 = majority. Even though the leader crashed before responding, the new leader election will pick a node with this entry, and it will be committed. The client may need to retry but the write is safe.

questions.q: During a Google Spanner-style deployment, you observe that cross-region transactions take 100-200ms even for simple writes. Your team asks why not just use Raft. What is the fundamental reason for this latency?
choices: TrueTime commit-wait: Spanner must wait out clock uncertainty (~7ms) plus cross-region Paxos round trips to guarantee external consistency
Spanner uses 2PC which is always slow
questions.explanation: Spanner's external consistency (real-time ordering) requires commit-wait: after a write, it waits for TrueTime uncertainty to pass so no future reader can see the write before its timestamp. Combined with cross-region Paxos consensus, this yields 100-200ms per write. This is the price of global strong consistency.

questions.q: A distributed lock service uses a 30-second TTL. Process A acquires the lock, then experiences a 35-second stop-the-world GC pause. Process B acquires the lock after TTL expires. A resumes and believes it still holds the lock. Both processes now write to shared storage. What should the storage layer enforce?
choices: Nothing — the lock service guarantees safety
Fencing tokens: reject writes from A because its token is older than B's
questions.explanation: This is Martin Kleppmann's classic argument against Redlock. Distributed locks with TTL alone cannot prevent this. The storage layer must validate monotonically increasing fencing tokens. Process B's token > A's token, so A's late writes are rejected. Without fencing, no TTL-based lock is truly safe.
